dialogue_exemplars: I was a posthumous child. My father's eyes had closed upon the light of this world six months, when mine opened on it.

"I looked at the stars, and considered
how awful it would be for a man to
turn his face to them as he froze to
death, and see no help or pity in
all the glittering multitude."
I didn't say that. Growing up in an orphanage, you're always lonely. You're just never alone.
Field mice girdle an apple tree. Pine mice kill the roots. I *know* what you have to do--you have to play God. Well... killing mice is as close as I want to come to playing God.
I've looked at so many women... I mean, I've seen *everything* about them, *everything*... but I never felt a thing. I felt nothing. Now... with you... it *hurts*... to look at you.
Good night, you Princes of Maine! You Kings of New England!

Thus I began my new life, in a new
name, and with everything new aboutme... I felt... like one in a dream...
The remembrance of that life is
fraught with so much... want of
hope... Whether it lasted for a year,
or more, or less, I do not know. I
only know that it was, and ceased to
be; and... there I leave it.
*Go* anywhere? Of course not! That would be *doing* something, wouldn't it? We wouldn't want to *do* something. Let's just sit here all night!
I'm not trying to be anything--I'm just doing nothing! If I wait and see long enough, then--with any luck--I won't *ever* have to make up my mind! Decisions can be painful, after all...
Mr. Rose, I'm in the *doctor* business.
No *almost* about it--I'm a doctor.

Henceforth you shall be... Little
Wilbur.

dialogue_exemplars: Here in St. Cloud's, I have come to understand that promises are rarely kept, that the battle isn't so much against evil as ignorance, and that being successful can't hold a candle to being of *use*.

Do I interfere? When absolutely helpless women tell me that they simply *can't* have an abortion, that they simply *must* go through with having another--and yet another--orphan... do I interfere? *Do* I? I do not. I do not even *recommend*. I just give them what they want: an orphan or an abortion.
My dear Homer, I thought you were over your adolescence, that period which I would define as the first time in our lives when we imagine we have something terrible to hide from those who love us.
God forgive me. I have *made* an orphan by loving him too much. Homer Wells will belong to St. Cloud's, forever.

It is strictly for our orphans' sake that I destroy any record of their natural mothers. Of course they will, one day, want to know. But orphans, especially, should look forward to their *futures*. Not back to their pasts.
Good night, you Princes of Maine! You Kings of New England!
Good night, you Princes of Maine! You Kings of New England.
One has to be more than "careful" of Mrs. Goodhall--she has sufficient Christian zeal to start her own country! I'd like to give her a little ether.

Even in the most enlightened times, unwanted babies will manage to be born. That there will always be orphans is simple not a problem to be solved. Here is St. Cloud's, we don't regard the sordid facts of life as problems.
Good night, you Princes of Maine, you Kings of New England!
You *bet* it's a heavy sedation! The fetus is unexpelled, her uterus is punctured, she has acute peritonitis, and there's a foreign object. I think it's a crochet hook.
He is a goddamn psychiatrist--of course he wants to "help"! He'd be happy if he could help *commit* me!
Sorry? I'm not 'sorry'! Not for anything I've done. I'm not even sorry that I love you!
Does it *snow* in Bombay? One winter here and we'll be shipping him south, in a *coffin*!
You'll have Princes of Maine! You'll have Kings of New England!

When absolutely helpless women tell me that they simply *can't* have an abortion, that they simply *must* go through with having another--and yet another--orphan... do I interfere? *Do* I? I do not. I do not even *recommend*. I just give them what they want: an orphan or an abortion.
My dear Homer, I thought you were over your adolescence, that period which I would define as the first time in our lives when we imagine we have something terrible to hide from those who love us.
God forgive me. I have *made* an orphan by loving him too much. Homer Wells will belong to St. Cloud's, forever.

It is strictly for our orphans' sake that I destroy any record of their natural mothers. Of course they will, one day, want to know. But orphans, especially, should look forward to their *futures*. Not back to their pasts.
Good night, you Princes of Maine! You Kings of New England!
Good night, you Princes of Maine! You Kings of New England.
One has to be more than "careful" of Mrs. Goodhall--she has sufficient Christian zeal to start her own country! I'd like to give her a little ether.

Even in the most enlightened times, unwanted babies will manage to be born. That there will always be orphans is simple not a problem to be solved. Here is St. Cloud's, we don't regard the sordid facts of life as problems.
Good night, you Princes of Maine, you Kings of New England!
You *bet* it's a heavy sedation! The fetus is unexpelled, her uterus is punctured, she has acute peritonitis, and there's a foreign object. I think it's a crochet hook.
He is a goddamn psychiatrist--of course he wants to "help"! He'd be happy if he could help *commit* me!
Sorry? I'm not 'sorry'! Not for anything I've done. I'm not even sorry that I love you!
Does it *snow* in Bombay? One winter here and we'll be shipping him south, in a *coffin*!
You'll have Princes of Maine! You'll have Kings of New England!

relation_context.relation_summary: The relationship between Dr. Larch and Homer Wells is multifaceted, rooted in a deep mentor-apprentice dynamic that evolves into a paternal and emotional bond. Larch assumes the role of both mentor and surrogate father, training Homer in obstetrics and abortions, instructing him from 'Gray's Anatomy,' and guiding him through medical procedures such as examinations, deliveries, and collaborative work on abortions. This professional apprenticeship is underscored by strong emotional undercurrents: Larch explicitly confesses his love for Homer in a voiceover, referring to him as 'my dear Homer' and calling him his 'work of art,' indicating profound affection and pride. Larch repeatedly protects Homer—from abusive foster parents and by fabricating a heart condition to shield him from the draft—demonstrating caregiving behavior that transcends professional duty. Their collaboration is evident in numerous shared medical events, including delivering babies, examining infants, setting up medical equipment, and discussing complex cases like Dorothy's uterus. Despite ideological differences about abortion, they maintain mutual respect: Larch acknowledges Homer's surgical skill and entrusts him with responsibilities, while Homer expresses gratitude by thanking Larch for his doctor's bag. However, the relationship is not without conflict. Tensions surface in events like the argument over the film splice, where they blame each other, and in moments of silent tension, such as when they stare at each other after a disagreement. These instances reveal underlying friction, possibly stemming from Homer's resistance to certain aspects of Larch's work. Nevertheless, their bond endures, culminating in a poignant farewell when Larch and the nurses wave Homer off, signifying both emotional attachment and acceptance of his independence. The relationship evolves from one-sided mentorship to a complex interplay of professional collaboration, emotional dependency, and reluctant release, profoundly shaping both characters—Homer’s moral development and eventual autonomy are direct outcomes of Larch’s guidance and protection.
